title_en: Distinctive Maturidi Positions in Tabsirat al-Adillah
title_ar: المسائل الماتريدية المميزة في تبصرة الأدلة
title_id: Posisi-Posisi Khas Maturidi dalam Tabsirat al-Adillah
content_en:
Tabsirat al-Adillah is particularly valuable for the clarity with which it identifies and defends the positions where the Maturidi school differs from the Ash'ari school. Understanding these differences requires understanding the shared framework: both schools use kalam methods, affirm the same core creedal positions, and defend a broadly Sunni theological orientation. The differences are real but occur against a background of substantial agreement.

The most significant difference concerns the role of reason in establishing moral and religious obligations. The Maturidi school holds that reason can independently establish certain truths about what is required of human beings — for instance, that gratitude to one's Creator is obligatory — even without a specific divine command making it so. The basis for this is the claim that reason can identify objective moral facts: certain actions are inherently good or bad, and reason can recognize this without being told by revelation. The Ash'ari school, by contrast, holds that moral obligations require divine command and that reason alone cannot establish what is obligatory or prohibited.

Abu Muin an-Nasafi defends the Maturidi position carefully in Tabsirat al-Adillah. He argues that the rational apprehension of moral truths does not infringe on divine sovereignty — God creates reason with the capacity to recognize these truths, and He also issues commands through revelation that reinforce and extend what reason can grasp. The relationship between rational and revealed moral knowledge is complementary, not competitive.

A second significant difference concerns the definition of faith. The Maturidi school holds that the essential component of faith is inner conviction (tasdiq), while verbal affirmation (iqrar) is a condition for being treated as a Muslim in worldly legal matters rather than a component of faith itself. A person who has genuine inner conviction but is unable to make verbal affirmation — due to muteness, extreme duress, or death before having the opportunity — is a believer. The Ash'ari school's position on this question is similar but formulated somewhat differently.

A third difference concerns the divine will and its relationship to human disobedience. The Maturidi school distinguishes between God's universal will (that all things occur according to His decree) and God's approving will (His pleasure with obedience and displeasure with disobedience). God does not will sin in the approving sense even though it occurs within His universal sovereignty. The Ash'ari formulation of this distinction differs in certain respects.

Abu Muin an-Nasafi's treatment of these differences in Tabsirat al-Adillah is even-handed but clear: he presents the Maturidi positions as superior on rational and scriptural grounds while acknowledging the sophistication of the Ash'ari formulations.
content_id:
Tabsirat al-Adillah sangat bernilai karena kejelasannya dalam mengidentifikasi dan membela posisi-posisi di mana mazhab Maturidi berbeda dari mazhab Asy'ari. Memahami perbedaan-perbedaan ini mengharuskan pemahaman atas kerangka bersama: kedua mazhab menggunakan metode kalam, menegaskan posisi aqidah inti yang sama, dan membela orientasi teologis Sunni secara luas. Perbedaan-perbedaannya nyata tetapi terjadi di atas latar belakang kesepakatan yang cukup besar.

Perbedaan paling signifikan menyangkut peran akal dalam menetapkan kewajiban moral dan keagamaan. Mazhab Maturidi berpendapat bahwa akal dapat secara mandiri menetapkan kebenaran-kebenaran tertentu tentang apa yang diwajibkan bagi manusia — misalnya, bahwa rasa syukur kepada Sang Pencipta adalah wajib — bahkan tanpa perintah ilahi yang secara khusus menetapkannya. Dasar dari ini adalah klaim bahwa akal dapat mengidentifikasi fakta-fakta moral yang objektif: tindakan-tindakan tertentu secara inheren baik atau buruk, dan akal dapat mengenali ini tanpa harus diberitahu oleh wahyu. Mazhab Asy'ari, sebaliknya, berpendapat bahwa kewajiban moral memerlukan perintah ilahi dan bahwa akal saja tidak dapat menetapkan apa yang wajib atau dilarang.

Abu Muin an-Nasafi membela posisi Maturidi dengan cermat dalam Tabsirat al-Adillah. Ia berargumen bahwa pemahaman rasional atas kebenaran-kebenaran moral tidak melanggar kedaulatan ilahi — Allah menciptakan akal dengan kapasitas untuk mengenali kebenaran-kebenaran ini, dan Dia juga mengeluarkan perintah melalui wahyu yang memperkuat dan memperluas apa yang dapat dipahami akal. Hubungan antara pengetahuan moral rasional dan yang diwahyukan bersifat saling melengkapi, bukan bersaingan.

Perbedaan signifikan kedua menyangkut definisi iman. Mazhab Maturidi berpendapat bahwa komponen esensial iman adalah keyakinan batin (tasdiq), sementara pengakuan verbal (iqrar) adalah syarat untuk diperlakukan sebagai Muslim dalam urusan hukum duniawi, bukan komponen iman itu sendiri. Seseorang yang memiliki keyakinan batin yang tulus tetapi tidak mampu mengucapkan pengakuan verbal — karena bisu, tekanan ekstrem, atau meninggal sebelum sempat melakukannya — adalah seorang mukmin. Posisi mazhab Asy'ari tentang persoalan ini serupa tetapi dirumuskan agak berbeda.

Perbedaan ketiga menyangkut kehendak ilahi dan hubungannya dengan kemaksiatan manusia. Mazhab Maturidi membedakan antara kehendak universal Allah (bahwa segala sesuatu terjadi sesuai ketetapan-Nya) dan kehendak ridha Allah (kesenangan-Nya atas ketaatan dan ketidaksenangan-Nya atas kemaksiatan). Allah tidak menghendaki dosa dalam pengertian ridha meskipun dosa terjadi dalam kedaulatan universal-Nya. Rumusan Asy'ari tentang pembedaan ini berbeda dalam beberapa hal.

Perlakuan Abu Muin an-Nasafi terhadap perbedaan-perbedaan ini dalam Tabsirat al-Adillah bersifat proporsional namun jelas: ia menyajikan posisi-posisi Maturidi sebagai lebih unggul berdasarkan landasan rasional dan skriptural, sambil mengakui kecanggihan rumusan-rumusan Asy'ari.
content_ar: Tabsirat al-Adillah is particularly valuable for the clarity with which it identifies and defends the positions where the الماتريدي school differs from the الأشعري school. الفهم these differences requires الفهم the shared framework: both schools use علم الكلام methods, affirm the same الأساسي creedal positions, and defend a broadly Sunni theological orientation. The differences are real but occur against a background of substantial agreement. The most المهم difference concerns the role of العقل in establishing moral and religious obligations. The الماتريدي school holds that العقل can independently establish certain truths about what is required of human beings — على سبيل المثال, that الشكر to one's Creator is obligatory — even without a specific divine command making it لذا. The basis for this is the claim that العقل can identify objective moral facts: certain actions are inherently الخير or bad, and العقل can recognize this without being told by الوحي. The الأشعري school, by contrast, holds that moral obligations require divine command and that العقل alone cannot establish what is obligatory or prohibited. Abu Muin an-Nasafi defends the الماتريدي position carefully in Tabsirat al-Adillah. He argues that the rational apprehension of moral truths does not infringe on divine sovereignty — God creates العقل with the capacity to recognize these truths, and He أيضاً issues commands through الوحي that reinforce and extend what العقل can grasp. The relationship between rational and revealed moral العلم is complementary, not competitive. A ثانياً المهم difference concerns the definition of الإيمان. The الماتريدي school holds that the الجوهري component of الإيمان is inner conviction (tasdiq), بينما verbal affirmation (iqrar) is a condition for being treated as a مسلم in worldly legal matters rather than a component of الإيمان itself. A person who has genuine inner conviction but is unable to make verbal affirmation — بسبب muteness, extreme duress, or الموت قبل having the opportunity — is a المؤمن. The الأشعري school's position on this question is similar but formulated somewhat differently. A ثالثاً difference concerns the divine will and its relationship to human disobedience. The الماتريدي school distinguishes between God's universal will (that all things occur وفقاً لـ His decree) and God's approving will (His pleasure with obedience and displeasure with disobedience). God does not will الذنب in the approving sense حتى وإن it occurs within His universal sovereignty. The الأشعري formulation of this distinction differs in certain respects. Abu Muin an-Nasafi's treatment of these differences in Tabsirat al-Adillah is even-handed but clear: he presents the الماتريدي positions as superior on rational and scriptural grounds بينما acknowledging the sophistication of the الأشعري formulations.
